comments.content:
It's time for Magic Monday again, my intermittent open post on occult theory and practice -- so ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight tonight Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What are the energetic properties of plastic/synthetic materials? Has anyone looked into this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Plastics are made from corpses -- the bodies of long-dead animals, which is where petroleum and natural gas come from. To me, and to many other people, that's what they feel like energetically as well. I suppose they'd be useful in necromancy, but I don't practice that, and I haven't found any other magical use for them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Can they be a magical hindrance, then?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on what you use them for. I wouldn't try making a talisman or a magical working tool out of plastic, certainly.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I am drawn to the Celtic GD, but very much want to learn from/with people, not alone with a book-- would being trained in the Hermetic GD first be a sensible approach? I am not keen on the aloof attitude to earth and matter and such, but I might be able to suspend my philosophical qualms.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's your choice, of course, but these days I strongly recommend a year of solitary practice from books before trying to find a group -- your chances of being able to find a worthwhile group go up substantially if you have some background in the work already. If you want to go in for training in a group first, it would be best to do something unrelated to the Golden Dawn tradition -- otherwise you'll have to unlearn much of what you learnt, as the system in The Celtic Golden Dawn has its own distinct way of doing things.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I asked partly because I like to learn things with the possibility of passing them on one day in mind, and that skill would be hard to get from books. Are the hermetic teaching methods transferable to the druidical GD?

(I already have the chance to join a worthwhile GD group, according to an expert opinion, not my own assessment)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The Druidical GD doesn't provide opportunities for teaching. It's arranged entirely to be learned and practiced on a solitary basis; the order basically functions as a venue for discussions of the teachings and practices, and a place to get your exams graded. When we have enough Druid grade initiates to begin chartering temples, those will have a variety of work to do, but students will still be responsible for their own training, following the curriculum in the book. I've set things up this way because I've watched too many magical lodges crash and burn because their members (a) didn't want to do the work the lodge was there to do, and (b) spent all their time playing social games of various kinds rather than doing the work the lodge was there to do.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I recently ran across the concept of a mage creating a faery-- are you familiar with this? What does it entail?

If faeries can be made, that would seem to have interesting implications regarding their nature...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: A mage can create an artificial elemental, and I wouldn't be surprised -- the intellectual sloppiness of pop occultism being what it is -- that somebody's confused artificial elementals and faeries. They aren't the same thing at all, and no, you can't create one.

comments.title: Travelling on the Astral plane
comments.content:
Hello!

In the open post of last week, you mentioned that to perform astral projections is not that difficult. Can you resume the technique for me please?

Are there any down side to it or dangers?

Thank You

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content:
You can find detailed instructions in dozens of books on magic -- one I recommend is Apprenticed to Magic by W.E. Butler. The very short form -- and you'll want to read at least one book on the subject before trying it -- is that you build up an image of your body in your imagination, as though it's standing a short distance in front of you, and you then transfer your consciousness to that image by an act of will. It takes practice, and exercises in concentration and visualization are good preparation.

The only real dangers you face in doing astral projection are getting too obsessive about it, on the one hand -- it takes a lot of energy to do, and you can exhaust yourself by doing it too much -- and mistaking astral perceptions for absolute truths on the other. I'll be covering that in this week's post on the blog.

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content: Are perceptions from etheric projection more accurate than those from astral projection?

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content: Perceptions of what? Your astral senses will accurately perceive the astral plane, your etheric senses will do the same on the etheric plane; neither one will perceive the physical plane clearly -- you need to use physical senses for that.

comments.title: Can you or a reader identify a book?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

For many years I've been looking for a book that my town library had in the mid-1980s. Given the breadth of your knowledge of literature, I'm hoping you (or perhaps another reader) might be able to identify it. The book was at least partly on the subject of a magical household. It had a chapter entitled "The Witch's Garden" or something similar, with nifty little diagrams as seen in gardening books of herbal and woody plantings for a magically inclined cottage garden, possibly printed in brown ink (though I may have confabulated that part over the years). I recall it as being a very large-format, but fairly thin, paperback. I'm pretty sure it was not one of Scott Cunningham's books. I will be very grateful for any suggestions. Thanks very much,

Dewey

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: Hmm! I don't know that book. Can anyone else help?

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: I thought at first it might be one of Pauline Campanelli's beautiful books, but what you describe isn't in any of them.  Nor does it seem to be in Harold A. Hansen's _The Witch's Garden_.  Could it have been Dorothy Jacob's _A Witch's Guide to Gardening_ (1965)?  I don't have that one, so I can't check it against your description.  She also wrote _Curss and Curses_ (1967), which I don't have either.  --  Robert Mathiesen

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: I just bought it (ouch, not cheap) - looks like it could be a neat book even if it's not the correct one.  (Sometimes I wonder if I imagined this book, but I don't think so.)  Thanks - Dewey

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: Do let us know here, once it has arrived, whether it was the right one. I am curious.  I'll check back weekly for a month or so.  --  Robert Mathiesen

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: Hi Robert - It wasn't the right one; it's a small hardback with a few older illustrations of single plants.  But it does look like it will be an interesting addition to my library.  Thanks!  - Dewey

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: Thanls so much for letting me know, Dewey!  I'm glad to hear that even so, you're happy with it.  --  RObert

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: perhaps it is Magical Herbalism: The Secret Craft of the Wise by Scott Cunningham. according to the review "The final portion of the book is a chapter on growing your own herb garden. Since this is a magickal herbal, you'll also find a few magickal methods to assist the growth of your herb garden in this chapter." review found at https://www.ecauldron.net/bkmh.php

replies.title: Re: Can you or a reader identify a book?
replies.content: Llewelyn published a book called "The Magical Household," by Scott Cunningham and David Harrington.

comments.title: Astral projection
comments.content: I have a question about astral projection. Many old school esoteric authors put a lot of emphasis on it (I'm thinking particularly here of W.E Butler who's works I've been re-reading) especially in the form of actually being able to step out of the physical body, see it lying on the bed/sitting on chair and then move around viewing the physical world. How important is this in your opinion in the style of magic you practice and do you have any hints to make exteriorisation (as Mouni Sadhu, another big fan, puts it) of achieving this?

replies.title: Re: Astral projection
replies.content: It's not of great importance in the system of magic I study and practice. As far as achieving it, Butler's as good a guide to that as I know of, and as I recall, he suggests regular practice and a lot of concentration and visualization exercises as helps to the process.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello John, I have read a number of comments concerning the Elemental Cross and the Sphere of Protection rituals. So here I go with my two cents worth. Cent number one. After faithfully performing both everyday for four months now, I have found that having the verbal portion of the ritual memorized so that I don't even have to think about it as I say it, allows me to focus much more on the symbolic aspect the of ritual. Actually it is almost as if someone else is reciting the verbal part, which makes the symbolic portion much more vivid and meaningful. Cent number two. I find that the ritual itself is, certainly on some days, rather routine and even mundane, but often the rest of my day seems to be spent in a heightened state of awareness. I do the ritual in the morning and I find this to be the real benefit as apposed to some whiz bang moment during the ritual itself.
Thanks for you time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Exactly. The point of magical practice is the effect it has on you over the long term, not the short term rush you get from doing it.

comments.title: emptiness
comments.content:
Thanks for hosting this again! I'm right smack dab in the middle of my Saturn Return. Life feels empty and I don't know know who I really am. Recently, I've started doing the SoP, and doing the meditation and divination routine you suggested on Well of Galabes.

Is there general advice you have you someone who is going through a Saturn return? Things that could help make this transit easier? Little spells or things to keep in mind? Any thoughts most appreciated!

replies.title: Re: emptiness
replies.content: Face your issues. That's what a Saturn return is all about, and the sooner you suck it up and confront the things Saturn wants you to confront, the easier it'll be. You may be able to get some advice by paying attention to aspects to Saturn in your natal chart, or other aspects Saturn is making to outer planets in the heavens at the time of your return -- but it really is a matter of gritting your teeth and doing what you have to do.

replies.title: Re: emptiness
replies.content:
Thank you for this. It's a bit overwhelming; Saturn is aspected with every planet, including the ascendent, in my natal chart! That being said I feel strangely fond towards Saturn, that is I try to embrace my limitations as I understand that all the ways I'm limited are conversely sources of great strength. Doing some cursory research shows much truth in the ways that I'm specifically limited by Saturn. I can acknowledge the painful issues in my life, and knowing those, work to transmute them. The prima materia is associated with Saturn, of course. What other substance do I have to transmute into gold, besides these things I have thrown out into the street and cast on to the dung-hill!

Well, walking with my sister today I found a beautiful squirrel that I dressed to make soup with later and an unopened bottle of beer which is a nice microbrew lager. I lift it now in a toast to gritting my teeth and confronting my issues!

replies.title: Re: emptiness
replies.content: If Saturn's that strongly connected in your chart, it's a very good idea to develop a good relationship with Saturnine energies!

comments.title: Learning Ritual Magic
comments.content:
Hi!

I just got Learning Ritual Magic and I'm about to start doing the practices. I go once a week to ashtanga yoga classes mainly for physical exercise. Is that a problem with the magical training?

Also English is not my native language. Do I translate the rituals myself, perform them in English or find more official translations in my own language?

Thanks in advance!

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content: Ashtanga yoga should be fine in combination with basic magical training of the kind taught in Learning Ritual Magic; if you go on to the more advanced forms of pranayama, that might become problematic, but if you're focusing on the physical-exercise end of yoga you should be fine. As for the rituals, you may do any of those things, depending on which you prefer -- as long as the names and words of power aren't changed, the rest can be put in any language you like.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Not a question, but I just wanted to say that after reading your post mentioning greeting the sun and the moon when you first see them each day, I took up the practice. Well, it's been a couple of weeks now and I experience a certain thrill every time I do it. If anything, it's getting stronger. The feeling is quite like when you're a teenager and you see the girl (or boy, no doubt) your crazy about, a kind of a flutter in the heart area. I don't know where, if anywhere, this will lead, but it's interesting and enjoyable so far, thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Glad to hear it. The feeling's a mild activation of the heart center, which is the energy center that has to do with relationship and connections between the self and the cosmos, so having that so quickly is a good sign

comments.title: Squaring off
comments.content: How do squares in the natal chart “turn into a source of enormous strength”? Some kind of spiritual isometrics?

replies.title: Re: Squaring off
replies.content: Through meditation, or simply by paying attention to your thoughts, feelings, and actions, you can learn to differentiate the two planetary energies and balance yourself between them. For example, I've got a Saturn-Mars square in my chart; by maintaining a conscious balance between the cold patient resistance of Saturn and the hot passionate dynamism of Mars, I can make use of either one at will. "The wise rule their stars, fools are ruled by theirs" -- it's an old adage and a good one.

replies.title: Re: Squaring off
replies.content:
Thank you for this. I also have Mars square Saturn, and I also wondered what you meant about squares being a source of strength.

I wondered if a Saturn talisman would be useful for me, despite the affliction, given his position (in my natal chart) in Libra. I think you answered that as well!

replies.title: Re: Squaring off
replies.content: Basically, to make use of an astrological talisman of a planet, that planet needs to have more dignities than debilities. In many of the old books -- Lilly's Christian Astrology is one good example -- there are numbers assigned to each dignity or debility; for example, a planet in the sign of its exaltation gets a +4, while a planet in the sign of its fall gets a -4. If you go through and add up all the dignities and debilities, and you come out with a positive number, you can make use of a talisman of that planet, and the higher the positive number the more powerful the talisman will be for you.

replies.title: Re: Squaring off
replies.content: Oh, I can see that. However when one has four planets conjunct squaring three planets conjunct the noise level gets pretty high, making it difficult to distinguish one 'voice' from another. Still, meditation is an option to try. Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Squaring off
replies.content: Actually, if they're conjunct, they're singing the same tune, so you can simply learn to distinguish the influence of one group from the influence of the other, and start with that. Later, through meditation and self-observation, you can learn to pick apart the individual influences, and play them against each other.

replies.title: Re: Squaring off
replies.content: Surely, I ought to play them WITH each other, as in creating a harmonious melody with chords. Is that not what one does with highly tensioned strings to make music of the spheres? (:-D

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
My grandmother (born around 1900) used to say not to plant parsley in your garden as it attracts pixies. When I was a kid that kind of appealed to me, but she exxplained that pixies cause mischief (and you don't want that, do you?)

Do you have any experience with parsley and/or pixies? Was this just nonsense from my old grandmother?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's not nonsense. More often, parsley is associated in magical tradition with death and the spirits of the dead, and it can bring some very problematic energies into your life unless you plant it with protective herbs.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: So what is the best magical companion plant for parsley? Mine is growing and reseeding itself and it's the only thing that survived the recent cold snap.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Chamomile. It's solar and protective, and in most temperate climates it does pretty well.

comments.title: Watcher at the Threshold
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
On your other blog, a commenter brought up the Watcher at the Threshold, which was the first time I'd seen the term, and I was wondering if you could speak more about it.
Is it a fairly common phenomenon? I've been slowly working through the Druid Magic Handbook for about two years now, and I have a hunch that I've encountered a version of the Watcher - but I don't want to get cocky.

replies.title: Re: Watcher at the Threshold
replies.content: The Watcher is a universal phenomenon; if you get involved in any spiritual path worth the name, you're going to run into it, probably much more than once. Magical theory has it that the Watcher is actually a downward projection of your own higher self, meant to keep you from starting on the path of awakening until you're actually ready to do so -- and you prove that you're ready by facing the Watcher, in whatever form it appears to you, instead of running like a rabbit.

replies.title: Re: Watcher at the Threshold
replies.content: Would it be accurate to say that the Watcher is faced successfully only by an act of will, and not by behaviors and attractions spurred by karma or destiny?

replies.title: Re: Watcher at the Threshold
replies.content: It would be entirely accurate to say that.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Our house has consistent, recurring, low-level plumbing issues. Each time we bring in a plumber to fix the apparent issue (a leaky pipe or a malodorous septic system), something else comes up (another small leak in a faucet, a corroded drain pipe). The issues are usually pretty minor, but they've come up enough that it's given me pause. Are there any possible or likely magical causes for this sort of thing - i.e. is the house (or something in or around it) trying to tell us something, and can we do anything gentle in the home-remedy way to address these issues?

I ask about gentle because we have a young baby at home, and I take seriously your advice about avoiding serious spiritual summoning or work in a home with young kids. Thanks!

replies.title: My husband thinks I'm nuts now
replies.content: I was going to comment above under "plastics", but this seems to be the perfect place for it.  A while back JMG suggested benefits to treating just about any kind of "non-living" object whatsoever as conscious.  At that time, our toilet was developing a problem where it runs after being flushed until you turn the cold water in the sink on/off, or if that doesn't work after several tries and associated cursing, take off the lid and bang on the float.  (It got this way once before and had to be fixed by a plumber.)  So I started thanking the thing whenever it behaved semi-well.  And it did, over a little time, start behaving a heck of a lot better.  The past week or so it's been getting flaky again, but not uber-flaky (the cold water trick works) so I continue to praise it for good behavior.  Hubby (who certainly believes in incorporeal consciousnesses, but apparently not porcelain ones) heard me talking to the toilet and laughed at me.  But said that he had done nothing previously to fix the thing.  So this is what reading JMG has led me to, chatting with toilets.

replies.title: Re: My husband thinks I'm nuts now
replies.content: Your husband will get used to it, especially when you begin to apply the same logic to other things in your life. There's a useful mystic acronym a lot of occultists used to use: TSW. It stands for "This S#!* Works" -- and it does.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Try pouring a small amount of Florida Water -- a cologne much used in traditional southern conjure -- down the drain every Friday, while asking the plumbing to behave better, or to let you know if there's something it's trying to tell you. Then pay attention to dreams and unexplained hunches -- the most likely forms in which the answer will come.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Could you describe extending that technique to a car? I talk to it like that already, and I do think I get better performance, but what would be the car equivalent of the Florida Water part? Splashing Florida Water on the tires?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have no idea, having never owned a car or had a driver's license.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for the advice.  I'll try that, and listen for any response.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Been following the CDG for almost two months now. The days I do it definitely go better than the days I don't. At first it was like switching diets and getting that slight euphoria of "wow this must be working" but now made it up to wind/purification meditations and I'm starting to notice some of the anger and frustration starting to leak out from around the edges of daily life again. Probably doing some purging here, but wanted to ask about the banishing ritual. The summoning is excellent for lack of energy but wondering if the banishing also works for cleaning up those embarrassing mental sewage spills.

Thanks Much
Ira Tibble

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, but of course there's more to it than that. Anger is very often a secondary emotion -- that is to say, it's something you learn to use so you don't have to feel another, more uncomfortable emotion. (For example, it's very common for children who grow up with a lot of fear in their lives to learn how to be angry instead, because that's less of a burden.) You might consider a course of reading in transactional analysis -- the books I'd specifically recommend are Games People Play, Scripts People Live, and Born to Win -- to help you sort out the unresolved emotional business in your life that's emerging in the form of anger and frustration.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Well of course you nailed it. Listened to most of the GPP audiobook while smashing through a welding project. Bad news is I'm not a special snowflake. Good news is I'll never need to buy another screenwriting book. Sincerest thanks again.

comments.title: gnostic celtic church protection ritual
comments.content:
JMG
About 2 years ago I adopted as best I could a ritual observance from your book, which I had to hand, Gnostic Celtic Church. This ritual seemed to generate protection for 'our grove', that is our family & dog, and our large plot with trees and other inhabitants. From the beginning I extended the Grove mostly to family scattered elsewhere in Britain.

I became aware from comments on your blogs that this ritual was different from the full Sphere of Protection available elsewhere, but persevered anyway with the /E/W/S/N visualization and with drawing down the vertical influence from the 'Sun', meeting the tellurium (?) moon influence from the Earth. Despite my personalized version - I still need my own approximate visualization - there seems, if very gradually, to be some help available. More recently 'the sun' seems to have helped specifically in a difficult repetitive dream - for which thanks. In a roundabout way the family connections have been worth attending to, including appreciation of some deceased members in particular an appreciation of my parents.

My question is really about the value of this rather limited exercise versus the perhaps more detailed other version(s), and the usefulness of my extending 'our grove' beyond the immediate location. I can easily visualize people creatures and locations, past and present.

best
Phil H

replies.title: Re: gnostic celtic church protection ritual
replies.content: The ritual as given in The Gnostic Celtic Church is the standard Sphere of Protection as taught in AODA and elsewhere, so you should be fine. (You might consider picking up a copy of The Druid Magic Handbook for more pointers on how to use it.) Yes, you can certainly use it to direct blessings and protections across distance; when I was the head of AODA we several times used the Sphere of Protection to direct healing energies at a member of the order who needed it, and had some remarkable cures as a result.

replies.title: Re: gnostic celtic church protection ritual
replies.content: Can pentagram rituals similarly be used at a distance?

replies.title: Re: gnostic celtic church protection ritual
replies.content: Not in my experience. The Sphere of Protection is a somewhat more flexible ritual.

replies.title: Re: gnostic celtic church protection ritual
replies.content:
Thanks a lot JMG for your reply and suggestion. My wording was a bit clumsy - I did not mean to imply the Gnostic Celtic Church version was inferior to others! I have stuck only to the Elemental Cross pp 31-32 and not used the (not-mandatory) divine names and symbols. There was/is not a lot of 'sphere' or 'rays of light shooting out'in my visualization and I have to make-do with approximations my imagination can manage.

But your reply has been a reassuring explanation.

best
Phil H

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm at the point in my Tarot practices where I'm considering designing or making my own deck. (I have basically no art skills, and while I'm aware that's not a total block, it does make it harder to get started.)

Do you have any suggestions about that? Good approaches; horrible approaches; definitely do this; skip it and stick with a deck you like if it's not clicking into place?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't really have any suggestions, as I've never tried that myself -- I've used a range of different decks, and when I get bored with one I usually just go try another.

comments.title: Prayer stuff mostly
comments.content:
1. What is happening when I do the proper rituals, prayers, and I feel nothing sometimes, whereas most times I feel Their presence. Is the God still listening, or taking a break, or have you experienced this? I’ve done meditation on this, and I feel they’re still listening, but it’s something to note.
2. Dealing with doubt. There’s apparently a phenomenon where if you stand on the edge of a cliff, you get intrusive thoughts like „I could definitely just jump off; what’s stopping me.” Sometimes, I get these sudden thoughts that magic isn’t real, or I get impious thoughts about the existence of the Gods. But then I shake my head realize the absurdity of those thoughts and go on my way. But their intrusiveness is unnerving. Doesn’t happen often, though. What’s going on here? Is it a holdover from my atheist past?

I know it’s a lot to ask, but I have to say I keep going back to my most favorite book of yours: A world full of Gods. Really happy I got it signed by you last year; just wish it was a bit longer ;)

Thanks for doing these magic mondays!

replies.title: Re: Prayer stuff mostly
replies.content: When you don't perceive them, it's you that's not perceiving them; they're still there. As for intrusive doubts, you're living in the middle of a society that's permeated with such thoughts -- it's not just you, it's the entire collective consciousness that surrounds you.

comments.title: Saturn Return/Perceptions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
My second Saturn Return started a couple weeks ago, and in that time an ancestral grandfather has made himself known to me, and I am grateful for that. My sense of myself seems also to expand several feet out from time to time, so it feels like I’m sort of watching myself from a distance. What may be up with that? And, as has been happening ever since I was initiated into Reiki II over a year ago, I feel the presence of several types of animals at various times of the day, generally around my SOP/meditation time in the morning, and distance Reiki time at night. I’m very fond of these animals, but haven’t a clue what they may be, other than my imagination. Anyway, I enjoy the sense of their presence, and thank them for coming. I’ve followed you long enough, though, to know there may be more to the story. As always, your insights are much appreciated.

(OtterGirl)

replies.title: Re: Saturn Return/Perceptions
replies.content: I haven't studied reiki, and it's possible that that's interacting with your magical work. The sense of watching yourself from a distance, though, is fairly common; you might try reading a book or two on the technique of astral projection and see if you can do it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Thank you for Magic Mondays!

In their original languages, were the names of gods and spirits also the word for what they represent/are made up of? If 'Zeus' was the word for sky, that would be a simplistic example of what I mean.

I know, this is way too general and cultures and languages and pantheons vary. But I imagine whatever you say will be interesting.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not usually, no. "Zeus" comes from an archaic word that apparently means something like "shining one," for example.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Can you elaborate more on the performance of magical ritual in a household where children live?  What types of operations should be avoided?  What are the potential consequences? At what age are children safe from untoward effects?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Anything that uses ceremonial means to call on anything other than a divinity should be avoided around children until they're past their seventh birthday. There's a risk of obsession or possession, depending on just what you're doing, and a much larger risk of leaving them vulnerable to unwelcome spiritual energies in less drastic forms.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When starting DOGD's ritual work, are there any suggested days and planetary hours for starting the first rituals?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No. The best time to start the first rituals is now, and the best place is wherever you are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you.

comments.title: Ghosts and other apparitions
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

The subject of ghosts came up on my blog and I was wondering whether peoples reactions to ghosts were different at points in the past than they are today? I became curious because one account described sisters trying to talk to a ghost at Borley Rectory, rather than running for their lives in fear (and inevitably becoming injured). The contrast was not lost on me! It is a bit like the reactions of one or two characters in certain Lovecraft novels, which you turned on its head in your own books!

Cheers

Chris

replies.title: Re: Ghosts and other apparitions
replies.content: Yep. People in many societies treat ancestral spirits as just an ordinary part of life; you make offerings to them, they help you out, everybody's happy.

comments.title: Gwyn
comments.content:
Am I too late?
I'm still working through Ovate grade in CGD and find it odd that the figures Gwyn and Coch have respectively water and air active.
However, air is of the Mind and forms and water is of the emotions, so given your meanings of both Gwyn and Coch, I'd imagine their active elements would be swapped - thoughts?

Also, you say the circumambulation at the end of the Rite of Rays is counter-clockwise so my intuition is to also do the water purification and fire consecration at the end also counter-clockwise. Im even inclined to do the closing pentagram counterclockwise with banishing. Is this ego or worth doing? Thoughts?

Many thanks for your guidance
Best

replies.title: Re: Gwyn
replies.content: You'd think so, wouldn't you? Meditate on them more and you'll see the point. As for the movements, I don't recommend it; the clockwise movements in the closing also have their reason for being there. Again, meditate on that and you'll see what the point is.

comments.title: Books!
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

As you know, I live in China. Books, and mail, from overseas often don’t make it through, which make me wary of ordering books, or to some extent even of enrolling on the OBOD course.

I will be flying back to the UK next week, and will be staying for a couple of weeks. It’ll be my best chance for who knows how long to stock up on books on Druidry.

You’ve covered a lot of really interesting topics recently: reincarnation, the ascent of the soul through different stages, the way mammals and plants etc have different paths, the different planes, the Welsh gods, and so on. Is there any one book, or two, which covers all of these? I have the Druidry Handbook, but it doesn’t go into the depth of your recent explanations.

Diolch yn fawr

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Books!
replies.content: Hmm. I don't know of one; I may have to write it one of these days.
